retrieved.text: Definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an architecture that enhances large language models by retrieving relevant external documents at inference time instead of relying solely on pre-trained knowledge. Key Components: - Document chunking: splitting documents into smaller segments - Embedding model: converting text into vector representations - Vector database: storing embeddings for efficient similarity search - Retriever: selecting top-k relevant chunks - Generator: producing answers based on retrieved context Advantages: - Access to up-to-date information - Ability to incorporate proprietary or domain-specific data - Reduced hallucination compared to pure LLM generation Limitations: - Strong dependence on retrieval quality - Errors in retrieval propagate to generation - Context window limits may restrict usable information Example Scenario: If a user asks about a specific regulation not present in the training data, RAG can retrieve the relevant document and generate a grounded response. Failure Case: If the retriever fails to find the relevant chunk, the model may generate incomplete or incorrect answers even when the knowledge exists in the corpus.

Claim 1: RAG significantly reduces hallucination because it grounds model outputs in retrieved factual documents. Claim 2: RAG does not eliminate hallucination entirely, especially when retrieval fails or retrieves irrelevant documents. Supporting Argument: Even when relevant documents exist in the corpus, poor chunking or similarity search errors can cause the system to retrieve incorrect context. Example: If a query is ambiguous, the retriever may select semantically similar but incorrect chunks, leading to misleading answers. Contradiction: Some studies claim that increasing top-k always improves performance, while others show that larger k introduces noise and reduces answer quality. Edge Case: When no relevant information exists in the corpus, the model may still generate an answer instead of admitting uncertainty. Implication: The effectiveness of RAG depends heavily on retrieval quality, chunking strategy, and prompt design rather than the LLM alone.

Module 7: RAG & Agentic Pipelines Production AI: An Engineering Guide Theja Tulabandhula University of Illinois Chicago 1 Overview RAG Foundations RAG Pipeline Development Agentic AI Systems Hands-On Demos Lab Exercise Assessments 2 Overview Module Overview How do AI assistants like Perplexity answer questions with up-to-date information they weren’t trained on? • Build Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems • Ground LLM outputs in external knowledge • Create multi-tool agent systems with intelligent routing • Implement observable decision traces for debugging 3 What You’ll Build 1. Complete RAG Pipeline • Document ingestion with chunking strategies • Vector search with FAISS/Chroma • Grounded generation with source attribution 2. Evaluation Framework • Retrieval precision, recall, MRR metrics • Generation faithfulness assessment 3. Multi-Tool Agent • RAG integration as callable tool • Observable decision traces 4 Module Roadmap RAG Foundations (Architecture, Chunking, Embeddings, Vector DBs) ↓ RAG Pipeline Development (Query Design, Context, Evaluation) ↓ Agentic AI Systems (Patterns, Multi-Tool, Agent Integration) 5 RAG Foundations RAG Architecture Overview What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation? • RAG = Architecture pattern enhancing LLM outputs with external knowledge • Retrieved at inference time, not encoded during training • Like an open-book exam : look up specific information when needed Why RAG Matters Problem RAG Solution Hallucination Ground responses in retrieved facts Knowledge cutoff Query current external data Proprietary data Access domain-specific knowledge bases ￿ See: RAG Architecture Overview 6 RAG Pipeline Architecture End-to-End Data Flow Documents → Chunking → Embedding → Vector Store ↓ User Query → Query Embedding → Similarity Search → Retrieved Context ↓ LLM + Context → Grounded Response Two Main Phases 1. Indexing Phase (offline): Process and store documents 2. Query Phase (online): Retrieve and generate responses ￿ See: RAG Architecture Overview 7 RAG Component Responsibilities Component Purpose Example Tools Document Loader Ingest raw documents LangChain loaders, PyPDF Text Splitter Chunk documents RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter Embedding Model Convert text to vectors sentence-transformers Vector Store Store and index embeddings FAISS, Chroma Retriever Find relevant chunks Similarity search, MMR LLM Generate grounded response vLLM, Ollama ￿ See: RAG Architecture Overview 8 Document Chunking Strategies Why Chunking Matters • Embedding models have token limits (256-512 tokens) • Entire documents dilute relevance signals • Chunks must be small enough for precision, large enough for coherence 9 Document Chunking Strategies (cont.) Common Strategies Strategy Best For Drawbacks Fixed-size Uniform processing Ignores semantic boundaries Recursive General documents May split mid-concept Sentence-based Precise retrieval Very small chunks Semantic (headers) Structured docs Requires formatting ￿ See: Document Chunking 10 Illustration: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter( chunk_size=512, # Max chars per chunk chunk_overlap=50, # Overlap between chunks separators=["

", "
", ". " , " " , ""], # Priority order length_function=len ) chunks = splitter.split_text(document_text) 11 Illustration: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (cont.) Key Parameters Parameter Typical Values chunk_size 256-1024 characters chunk_overlap 10-20% of chunk_size separators Paragraphs → Sentences → Words ￿ See: Document Chunking 12 Embedding Models What are Embeddings? • Dense numerical vectors (384-1536 dimensions) capturing semantic meaning • Semantically similar texts imply geometrically close vectors • “automobile” matches “car” even without exact word overlap Popular Models Model Dimensions Speed

results.answer: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an architecture that enhances large language models by retrieving relevant external documents at inference time instead of relying solely on pre-trained knowledge. This approach allows access to up-to-date information and can incorporate proprietary or domain-specific data, reducing hallucination compared to pure LLM generation but still dependent on retrieval quality. Insufficient evidence in retrieved context (for conflict resolution).

results.query: What are the main components of a RAG system?

retrieved.text: Homework 7.2: Compare Three RAG Architectures on Groundedness MLOps Course - Module 7 Aligned Learning Objectives • CG3.LO3: Evaluate alternative RAG design patterns. • CG3.LO4: Analyze how architecture choices affect reasoning and output quality . Why This Matters Not all RAG systems are created equal. Different architectural choices-such as query rewriting, reranking, or agentic decomposition-can dramatically impact retrieval quality , response accuracy , and computational cost. Understanding these trade-offs is essential for designing production RAG systems that balance performance, latency , and resource constraints. In this assignment, you will implement and compare three distinct RAG architectures to evaluate their groundedness and accuracy . This hands-on comparison will deepen your understanding of how architectural decisions affect real-world system behavior- a critical skill for MLOps practitioners working with knowledge-grounded AI systems. Assignment Overview You will implement three distinct RAG configurations and systematically compare their performance on groundedness and accuracy using the same dataset and query set. The goal is to understand how different architectural patterns affect retrieval quality , latency , and output reliability . Example RAG architectures to consider: • Naive RAG: Top-k retrieval → direct generation • RAG with query rewriting: Query expansion or reformulation before retrieval • RAG with reranking: Initial retrieval followed by semantic reranking • RAG with agentic decomposition: Multi-step retrieval guided by task plan- ning You may choose any three architectures that differ meaningfully in their approach to retrieval and generation. 1 Deliverables You must submit the following components: 1. Three RAG implementations • Each implementation must use a distinct architectural pattern • Implementations may share code for ingestion/indexing to ensure fair com- parison • Document the key differences between architectures 2. Groundedness evaluation using a rubric you define • Create clear criteria for measuring groundedness (e.g., citation accuracy , factual consistency , hallucination rate) • Apply your rubric consistently across all three architectures • Document your evaluation methodology 3. Summary comparison table including: • Retrieval quality (precision, recall, or custom metrics) • Latency (end-to-end response time) • Grounding percentage (how often responses are properly grounded in retrieved context) • Failure types (categorization of errors for each architecture) 4. Analysis report (1-2 pages) addressing: • Which architecture is most robust and why • Trade-offs between architectures (speed vs. accuracy , complexity vs. relia- bility) • Recommendations for when to use each architecture • Insights from your evaluation Requirements & Constraints • Architectures must differ meaningfully -not just trivial parameter variations • Use any open-source embeddings/vector index (FAISS, Chroma, Weaviate, etc.) • Test all architectures on the same dataset and query set for fair comparison • Document your experimental setup for reproducibility • Provide clear justification for your groundedness evaluation criteria Rubric (10 pts) 2 Criteria Points Description Correct implementation of three distinct RAG architectures 3 Each architecture is properly implemented with meaningful differences; code is functional and well-documented Evaluation design + depth of groundedness metrics 3 Groundedness rubric is well-defined, consistently applied, and captures meaningful quality differences Insightfulness of comparison analysis 2 Analysis demonstrates deep understanding of architectural trade-offs and provides actionable insights Quality of result tables/visualizations 1

Claim 1: RAG significantly reduces hallucination because it grounds model outputs in retrieved factual documents. Claim 2: RAG does not eliminate hallucination entirely, especially when retrieval fails or retrieves irrelevant documents. Supporting Argument: Even when relevant documents exist in the corpus, poor chunking or similarity search errors can cause the system to retrieve incorrect context. Example: If a query is ambiguous, the retriever may select semantically similar but incorrect chunks, leading to misleading answers. Contradiction: Some studies claim that increasing top-k always improves performance, while others show that larger k introduces noise and reduces answer quality. Edge Case: When no relevant information exists in the corpus, the model may still generate an answer instead of admitting uncertainty. Implication: The effectiveness of RAG depends heavily on retrieval quality, chunking strategy, and prompt design rather than the LLM alone.

Definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an architecture that enhances large language models by retrieving relevant external documents at inference time instead of relying solely on pre-trained knowledge. Key Components: - Document chunking: splitting documents into smaller segments - Embedding model: converting text into vector representations - Vector database: storing embeddings for efficient similarity search - Retriever: selecting top-k relevant chunks - Generator: producing answers based on retrieved context Advantages: - Access to up-to-date information - Ability to incorporate proprietary or domain-specific data - Reduced hallucination compared to pure LLM generation Limitations: - Strong dependence on retrieval quality - Errors in retrieval propagate to generation - Context window limits may restrict usable information Example Scenario: If a user asks about a specific regulation not present in the training data, RAG can retrieve the relevant document and generate a grounded response. Failure Case: If the retriever fails to find the relevant chunk, the model may generate incomplete or incorrect answers even when the knowledge exists in the corpus.

results.answer:
The main components of a RAG system are:
- Document chunking
- Embedding model
- Vector database
- Retriever
- Generator

These components work together to enhance large language models by retrieving relevant external documents at inference time, improving grounding and reducing hallucination. (chunk_38)

Homework 7.2: Compare Three RAG Architectures on Groundedness MLOps Course - Module 7 Aligned Learning Objectives • CG3.LO3: Evaluate alternative RAG design patterns. • CG3.LO4: Analyze how architecture choices affect reasoning and output quality . Why This Matters Not all RAG systems are created equal. Different architectural choices-such as query rewriting, reranking, or agentic decomposition-can dramatically impact retrieval quality , response accuracy , and computational cost. Understanding these trade-offs is essential for designing production RAG systems that balance performance, latency , and resource constraints. In this assignment, you will implement and compare three distinct RAG architectures to evaluate their groundedness and accuracy . This hands-on comparison will deepen your understanding of how architectural decisions affect real-world system behavior- a critical skill for MLOps practitioners working with knowledge-grounded AI systems. Assignment Overview You will implement three distinct RAG configurations and systematically compare their performance on groundedness and accuracy using the same dataset and query set. The goal is to understand how different architectural patterns affect retrieval quality , latency , and output reliability . Example RAG architectures to consider: • Naive RAG: Top-k retrieval → direct generation • RAG with query rewriting: Query expansion or reformulation before retrieval • RAG with reranking: Initial retrieval followed by semantic reranking • RAG with agentic decomposition: Multi-step retrieval guided by task plan- ning You may choose any three architectures that differ meaningfully in their approach to retrieval and generation. 1 Deliverables You must submit the following components: 1. Three RAG implementations • Each implementation must use a distinct architectural pattern • Implementations may share code for ingestion/indexing to ensure fair com- parison • Document the key differences between architectures 2. Groundedness evaluation using a rubric you define • Create clear criteria for measuring groundedness (e.g., citation accuracy , factual consistency , hallucination rate) • Apply your rubric consistently across all three architectures • Document your evaluation methodology 3. Summary comparison table including: • Retrieval quality (precision, recall, or custom metrics) • Latency (end-to-end response time) • Grounding percentage (how often responses are properly grounded in retrieved context) • Failure types (categorization of errors for each architecture) 4. Analysis report (1-2 pages) addressing: • Which architecture is most robust and why • Trade-offs between architectures (speed vs. accuracy , complexity vs. relia- bility) • Recommendations for when to use each architecture • Insights from your evaluation Requirements & Constraints • Architectures must differ meaningfully -not just trivial parameter variations • Use any open-source embeddings/vector index (FAISS, Chroma, Weaviate, etc.) • Test all architectures on the same dataset and query set for fair comparison • Document your experimental setup for reproducibility • Provide clear justification for your groundedness evaluation criteria Rubric (10 pts) 2 Criteria Points Description Correct implementation of three distinct RAG architectures 3 Each architecture is properly implemented with meaningful differences; code is functional and well-documented Evaluation design + depth of groundedness metrics 3 Groundedness rubric is well-defined, consistently applied, and captures meaningful quality differences Insightfulness of comparison analysis 2 Analysis demonstrates deep understanding of architectural trade-offs and provides actionable insights Quality of result tables/visualizations 1

results.answer: A retriever in RAG selects top-k relevant chunks from a vector database based on the query. However, its effectiveness depends heavily on retrieval quality; poor retrieval can lead to hallucination or incorrect answers even when relevant information exists (Chunk IDs: 0, 38).

results.answer: Embeddings in RAG convert text into vector representations, enabling efficient similarity search in a vector database. This step is crucial for selecting relevant chunks of information to ground the model's responses (chunk_38). However, the quality of retrieval can be affected by factors such as chunking strategy and similarity search errors, which may introduce noise or incorrect context (chunk_0).

Definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an architecture that enhances large language models by retrieving relevant external documents at inference time instead of relying solely on pre-trained knowledge. Key Components: - Document chunking: splitting documents into smaller segments - Embedding model: converting text into vector representations - Vector database: storing embeddings for efficient similarity search - Retriever: selecting top-k relevant chunks - Generator: producing answers based on retrieved context Advantages: - Access to up-to-date information - Ability to incorporate proprietary or domain-specific data - Reduced hallucination compared to pure LLM generation Limitations: - Strong dependence on retrieval quality - Errors in retrieval propagate to generation - Context window limits may restrict usable information Example Scenario: If a user asks about a specific regulation not present in the training data, RAG can retrieve the relevant document and generate a grounded response. results.answer: The limitations of RAG systems include strong dependence on retrieval quality, where errors in retrieval can propagate to generation, and context window limits that may restrict usable information. Additionally, while RAG reduces hallucination compared to pure LLMs, it is not a complete solution as poor chunking or similarity search errors can still lead to misleading answers (Chunk IDs: 38, 0).

Why This Matters Not all RAG systems are created equal. Different architectural choices-such as query rewriting, reranking, or agentic decomposition-can dramatically impact retrieval quality , response accuracy , and computational cost. Understanding these trade-offs is essential for designing production RAG systems that balance performance, latency , and resource constraints. In this assignment, you will implement and compare three distinct RAG architectures to evaluate their groundedness and accuracy . This hands-on comparison will deepen your understanding of how architectural decisions affect real-world system behavior- a critical skill for MLOps practitioners working with knowledge-grounded AI systems. Assignment Overview You will implement three distinct RAG configurations and systematically compare their performance on groundedness and accuracy using the same dataset and query set. The goal is to understand how different architectural patterns affect retrieval quality , latency , and output reliability . Example RAG architectures to consider: • Naive RAG: Top-k retrieval → direct generation • RAG with query rewriting: Query expansion or reformulation before retrieval • RAG with reranking: Initial retrieval followed by semantic reranking • RAG with agentic decomposition: Multi-step retrieval guided by task plan- ning You may choose any three architectures that differ meaningfully in their approach to retrieval and generation. 1 Deliverables You must submit the following components: 1. Three RAG implementations • Each implementation must use a distinct architectural pattern • Implementations may share code for ingestion/indexing to ensure fair com- parison • Document the key differences between architectures 2. Groundedness evaluation using a rubric you define • Create clear criteria for measuring groundedness (e.g., citation accuracy , factual consistency , hallucination rate) • Apply your rubric consistently across all three architectures • Document your evaluation methodology 3. Summary comparison table including: • Retrieval quality (precision, recall, or custom metrics) • Latency (end-to-end response time) • Grounding percentage (how often responses are properly grounded in retrieved context) • Failure types (categorization of errors for each architecture) 4. Analysis report (1-2 pages) addressing: • Which architecture is most robust and why • Trade-offs between architectures (speed vs. accuracy , complexity vs. relia- bility) • Recommendations for when to use each architecture • Insights from your evaluation Requirements & Constraints • Architectures must differ meaningfully -not just trivial parameter variations • Use any open-source embeddings/vector index (FAISS, Chroma, Weaviate, etc.) • Test all architectures on the same dataset and query set for fair comparison • Document your experimental setup for reproducibility • Provide clear justification for your groundedness evaluation criteria Rubric (10 pts) 2 Criteria Points Description Correct implementation of three distinct RAG architectures 3 Each architecture is properly implemented with meaningful differences; code is functional and well-documented Evaluation design + depth of groundedness metrics 3 Groundedness rubric is well-defined, consistently applied, and captures meaningful quality differences Insightfulness of comparison analysis 2 Analysis demonstrates deep understanding of architectural trade-offs and provides actionable insights Quality of result tables/visualizations 1

results.answer: RAG can fail even when relevant information exists due to poor chunking or similarity search errors leading to the retrieval of incorrect context. This issue is exacerbated by ambiguous queries, which may cause the retriever to select semantically similar but incorrect chunks (Chunk IDs: 0, 38). Insufficient evidence in retrieved context.

results.query: What are common retrieval failure modes in RAG?

retrieved.text: Claim 1: RAG significantly reduces hallucination because it grounds model outputs in retrieved factual documents. Claim 2: RAG does not eliminate hallucination entirely, especially when retrieval fails or retrieves irrelevant documents. Supporting Argument: Even when relevant documents exist in the corpus, poor chunking or similarity search errors can cause the system to retrieve incorrect context. Example: If a query is ambiguous, the retriever may select semantically similar but incorrect chunks, leading to misleading answers. Contradiction: Some studies claim that increasing top-k always improves performance, while others show that larger k introduces noise and reduces answer quality. Edge Case: When no relevant information exists in the corpus, the model may still generate an answer instead of admitting uncertainty. Implication: The effectiveness of RAG depends heavily on retrieval quality, chunking strategy, and prompt design rather than the LLM alone.

Homework 7.1: Retrieval Stress-Test & Failure-Mode Analysis MLOps Course - Module 7 Aligned Learning Objectives • CG3.LO3: Diagnose weaknesses in retrieval systems. • CG3.LO4: Analyze how agentic systems fail when retrieval is incorrect. Why This Matters Retrieval systems are the foundation of RAG pipelines and agentic AI workflows. How- ever , they often fail silently-returning irrelevant chunks, missing critical context, or collapsing under ambiguous queries. Understanding these failure modes is essential for building robust production systems. In this assignment, you will intentionally stress-test a RAG system to expose its weaknesses and develop strategies to mitigate them. This skill is critical for diagnosing production issues and improving system reliability in real-world MLOps environments. Assignment Overview You will intentionally push a RAG system to failure by designing adversarial and edge- case queries that expose retrieval weaknesses. Your goal is to characterize error modes-such as ambiguous queries, adversarial phrasing, long-context queries, and missing facts-and analyze their downstream impact on reasoning or tool selection in agentic systems. This assignment emphasizes understanding brittle retrieval behavior and document- ing systematic failure patterns that can inform future system improvements. Deliverables You must submit the following components: 1. Stress-test suite containing 20+ queries specifically designed to break re- trieval 1 • Include diverse failure scenarios (ambiguous queries, adversarial phrasing, long-context queries, missing facts, etc.) • Document the intent behind each query type 2. Execution logs showing: • Retrieval outputs for each query • Model outputs/responses • Clear labeling of which queries triggered failures 3. Failure taxonomy describing at least 4 types of failure , such as: • Semantic drift (query meaning shifts during retrieval) • Nearest-neighbor collapse (all results cluster around irrelevant matches) • Chunk boundary errors (relevant information split across chunks) • Missing context (facts not present in knowledge base) • Other failure modes you discover 4. Mitigation proposal (1 page) listing: • Specific strategies to address each failure type • Feasibility assessment of proposed solutions • Trade-offs between different mitigation approaches Requirements & Constraints • You must demonstrate failures ; you are not required to fix them in this assignment • Your retrieval engine must be swappable -do not hard-code to a specific library • Use any open-source retrieval framework (FAISS, Chroma, Weaviate, etc.) • Document your experimental setup clearly for reproducibility Rubric (10 pts) Criteria Points Description Quality and diversity of stress-test queries 3 Queries effectively expose different failure modes; demonstrate creative adversarial thinking Correct identification + labeling of failure modes 3 Failure taxonomy is accurate, well-documented, and covers distinct error types Discussion of downstream consequences for agentic systems 2 Clear analysis of how retrieval failures impact reasoning, tool selection, or task completion 2 Criteria Points Description Clarity of logs/evidence 1 Execution logs are well-organized and clearly demonstrate failures Feasibility of mitigation recommendations 1 Proposed solutions are practical, specific, and grounded in retrieval system design T otal: 10 points What Success Looks Like A high-quality submission will: • Demonstrate systematic thinking about retrieval failure modes • Include diverse, creative queries that expose different weaknesses • Provide clear evidence (logs) showing exactly how and why the system failed

Definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an architecture that enhances large language models by retrieving relevant external documents at inference time instead of relying solely on pre-trained knowledge. Key Components: - Document chunking: splitting documents into smaller segments - Embedding model: converting text into vector representations - Vector database: storing embeddings for efficient similarity search - Retriever: selecting top-k relevant chunks - Generator: producing answers based on retrieved context Advantages: - Access to up-to-date information - Ability to incorporate proprietary or domain-specific data - Reduced hallucination compared to pure LLM generation Limitations: - Strong dependence on retrieval quality - Errors in retrieval propagate to generation - Context window limits may restrict usable information Example Scenario: If a user asks about a specific regulation not present in the training data, RAG can retrieve the relevant document and generate a grounded response. Failure Case: If the retriever fails to find the relevant chunk, the model may generate incomplete or incorrect answers even when the knowledge exists in the corpus.

results.answer: Common retrieval failure modes in RAG include retrieving irrelevant documents, semantic drift during retrieval, nearest-neighbor collapse, and chunk boundary errors. These issues can lead to misleading answers and reduced answer quality. The effectiveness of RAG heavily depends on retrieval quality, chunking strategy, and prompt design (Chunk IDs: 0, 38).

results.query: How do architecture choices affect groundedness in RAG?

Why This Matters Not all RAG systems are created equal. Different architectural choices-such as query rewriting, reranking, or agentic decomposition-can dramatically impact retrieval quality , response accuracy , and computational cost. Example RAG architectures to consider: • Naive RAG: Top-k retrieval → direct generation • RAG with query rewriting: Query expansion or reformulation before retrieval • RAG with reranking: Initial retrieval followed by semantic reranking • RAG with agentic decomposition: Multi-step retrieval guided by task plan- ning You may choose any three architectures that differ meaningfully in their approach to retrieval and generation. 1 Deliverables You must submit the following components: 1. code is functional and well-documented Evaluation design + depth of groundedness metrics 3 Groundedness rubric is well-defined, consistently applied, and captures meaningful quality differences Insightfulness of comparison analysis 2 Analysis demonstrates deep understanding of architectural trade-offs and provides actionable insights Quality of result tables/visualizations 1 Results are clearly presented with appropriate tables, charts, or visualizations Environment reproducibility 1 Complete environment setup with clear instructions for running all three implementations T otal: 10 points What Success Looks Like A high-quality submission will: • Implement three architecturally distinct RAG systems with clear documentation • Define and apply a rigorous groundedness evaluation methodology • Present comprehensive comparison data with clear visualizations • Provide insightful analysis of when and why each architecture excels or fails • Include reproducible code with complete environment specifications Tips for Success • Start simple: Implement naive RAG first, then add complexity incrementally • Use consistent test data: Ensure all architectures are evaluated on identical queries and documents • Define groundedness clearly: Create specific, measurable criteria (e.g., “re- sponse contains direct quote from retrieved chunk”) • Measure multiple dimensions: Don’t just focus on accuracy-consider latency , cost, and failure modes 3 • Document design decisions: Explain why you chose specific architectures and evaluation metrics • Look for patterns: Identify which types of queries favor which architectures • Consider edge cases: Test with ambiguous queries, missing information, and contradictory sources Challenge Extensions (Optional) Want to go further? Consider these optional enhancements: • Cost modeling: Incorporate compute time, memory footprint, and API costs into your comparison • Hybrid architecture: Design a fourth system that combines strengths of mul- tiple approaches • Automated architecture selection: Build a router that chooses the best archi- tecture based on query characteristics • Failure analysis: Deep-dive into specific failure cases and propose targeted improvements Submission Requirements Submit the following files: 1. Three RAG implementation files (Python scripts or notebooks) 2. Shared ingestion/indexing code (if applicable) 3. Evaluation dataset and queries (JSON, CSV , or similar format) 4. Claim 1: RAG significantly reduces hallucination because it grounds model outputs in retrieved factual documents. Claim 2: RAG does not eliminate hallucination entirely, especially when retrieval fails or retrieves irrelevant documents. Supporting Argument: Even when relevant documents exist in the corpus, poor chunking or similarity search errors can cause the system to retrieve incorrect context. Example: If a query is ambiguous, the retriever may select semantically similar but incorrect chunks, leading to misleading answers. Contradiction: Some studies claim that increasing top-k always improves performance, while others show that larger k introduces noise and reduces answer quality. Edge Case: When no relevant information exists in the corpus, the model may still generate an answer instead of admitting uncertainty. Implication: The effectiveness of RAG depends heavily on retrieval quality, chunking strategy, and prompt design rather than the LLM alone.

results.answer: Architecture choices significantly impact groundedness in RAG systems. Different strategies like query rewriting, reranking, or agentic decomposition affect retrieval quality, response accuracy, and computational cost (chunk_3). However, even with good retrievals, hallucination can occur due to poor chunking or similarity search errors (chunk_0), highlighting the importance of robust architectural design and evaluation methodologies.

results.query: How should an agent use retrieval in a multi-step workflow?

retrieved.text: self.pipeline = rag_pipeline self.k = k def __call__(self, query: str) -> dict: result = self.pipeline.query(query, k =self.k) return { "answer": result[ "response"], "sources": result[ "sources"], "latency_ms": result[ "latency"]["total_ms"] } # See next slide 76 Retrieval-Agent Integration: Wrapping RAG as a Tool (cont.) # Class continued from previous slide @property def description(self) -> str: return ("Search knowledge base for facts, definitions, " "or how-to instructions from documents." ) ￿ See: Retrieval Agent Integration 77 Building the Agent Controller class RetrievalAgent: def __init__(self, tools: dict[ToolName, Tool]): self.tools = tools self.context_buffer = "" def run(self, query: str) -> tuple[str, AgentTrace]: trace = AgentTrace(query=query) # Route to appropriate tool trace.add_step("thought", f"Analyzing query: ' {query}'") selected_tool = self._route_query(query, trace) # Function continues in the next slide 78 Building the Agent Controller # run() function continued from previous slide # Execute selected tool trace.add_step("action", { "tool": selected_tool.value, "input": query})↪ result = self.tools[selected_tool].execute(query) trace.add_step("observation", { "output": result.output}) trace.final_answer = result.output.get("answer", str(result.output)) return trace.final_answer, trace ￿ See: Retrieval Agent Integration 79 Agent Evaluation & Tracing Why Tracing Matters • Agents are diﬀicult to debug without comprehensive tracing • Need to identify if failure came from routing, tool execution , or synthesis • Traces answer: “Why did the agent choose this tool?” 80 Agent Evaluation & Tracing (cont.) Trace Structure @dataclass class AgentTrace: trace_id: str query: str steps: list[TraceStep] # thought, action, observation, error final_answer: Optional[str] success: bool total_duration_ms: float ￿ See: Agent Evaluation & Tracing 81 What to Inspect in an Agent Trace Inspection Question Why It Matters Was the right tool selected? Distinguishes routing errors from tool failures Did retrieved evidence materially help? Confirms retrieval is useful, not decorative Did the agent loop unnecessarily? Reveals weak stopping logic or poor planning Did a guardrail fire when it should? Tests safety and escalation behavior • These are the same questions students should answer in Milestone 6 trace analysis • A good trace supports debugging, evaluation, and governance 82 Agent Evaluation Metrics def calculate_success_rate(traces: list[AgentTrace]) -> float: """Fraction of tasks completed successfully.""" return sum(1 for t in traces if t.success) / len(traces) def calculate_step_efficiency(traces: list, max_expected: int = 3): """Analyze step counts relative to expected.""" step_counts = [len(t.steps) for t in traces] return { "avg_steps": sum(step_counts) / len(step_counts), "under_budget": sum( 1 for c in step_counts if c <= max_expected) / len(step_counts)↪ } 83 Agent Evaluation Metrics (cont.) Dimension What It Measures Metrics Task Success Did agent complete? Success rate Decision Quality Right tool selected? Tool accuracy Eﬀiciency Steps needed? Avg steps Latency Response time? p50, p95, max ￿ See: Agent Evaluation & Tracing 84 Hands-On Demos MWE: Embedding Similarity Demo Overview Explore how embedding models represent text and how similarity metrics affect retrieval. cd mod7-code/mwe/embedding-similarity pip install -r requirements.txt python main.py Key Concepts Demonstrated Concept Implementation Cosine Similarity cosine_similarity() — dot product / magnitudes L2 Distance l2_distance() — Euclidean norm of difference Semantic Matching Synonym pairs with zero keyword overlap Batch Encoding model.encode(texts, batch_size=32) Cross-Domain Clustering Domain similarity matrix 85 MWE: Embedding Similarity Output Demo 2: Semantic vs Keyword Matching Text A Text B Cosine Keyword automobile car 0.721 0.000 ←

calling an API in _mock_reason() • Implement retry logic when a tool fails (try alternative tool) • Build a planning step before the ReAct loop (Plan-then-Act) 96 MWE: Multi-Tool Agent Demo Overview Agent controller selecting between tools based on query intent. cd src/module7/mwe/multi-tool-agent python main.py # No external dependencies Components Demonstrated Component Implementation Tool abstraction Callable tools with descriptions Heuristic routing Keyword-based tool selection Context passing Storing retrieval for summarization Execution tracing Capturing each step 97 MWE: Multi-Tool Agent Trace # Routing Logic - Summarize keywords → SummarizeTool ( if context exists) - Question keywords → RetrievalTool - Fallback → ClarifyTool 98 MWE: Multi-Tool Agent Trace (cont.) # Trace Structure { "query": "What is RAG?" , "steps": [ {"type": "thought", "content": "Analyzing query..." }, {"type": "routing", "decision": "retrieval"}, {"type": "action", "tool": "retrieval", "input": "What is RAG?" }, {"type": "observation", "output": {...}}, {"type": "answer", "final_answer": "RAG is..." } ], "tool_sequence": [ "retrieval"] } 99 Lab Exercise Lab: RAG Pipeline Fundamentals Learning Objectives • LO1: Implement document chunking with configurable parameters • LO2: Generate embeddings and store in FAISS vector index • LO3: Perform similarity search to retrieve relevant chunks • LO4: Create grounded prompts combining query and context • LO5: Measure latency at each pipeline stage Time Estimate: ~30 minutes 100 Lab: Key Exercises TODO 1: Document Chunking def chunk_documents(documents, max_chunk_size =300, overlap =50): # Split into paragraphs, then by sentence if needed # Preserve metadata for each chunk pass TODO 2-6: Full Pipeline • Generate embeddings with sentence-transformers • Create FAISS index • Implement similarity search • Create grounded prompts • Build complete RAGPipeline class with timing 101 Assessments Milestone 6: RAG & Agentic Pipeline Part 1: RAG Pipeline (5 points) • Document ingestion with chunking strategy • Embedding generation and vector index • Retrieval and grounded generation • Evaluation report on 10 queries: precision@k, recall@k, groundedness • Pipeline diagram and latency measurements Part 2: Multi-Tool Agent (5 points) • At least 2 tools (retriever + one additional) • Tool selection policy (documented) • 10 multi-step evaluation tasks with traces • Observable routing decisions Total: 10 points 102 Homework Assignments HW 7.1: Retrieval Stress-Test (10 pts) • Design 20+ queries to break retrieval • Failure taxonomy (4+ failure types) • Mitigation proposals HW 7.2: RAG Architecture Comparison (10 pts) • Implement 3 distinct RAG architectures • Compare groundedness and latency • Analyze trade-offs HW 7.3: Agentic Task Decomposition (10 pts) • Retrieval-guided planning module • Multi-step execution with evidence tracing • 5 complex evaluation questions 103 Key Takeaways 1. RAG Architecture • Combine retrieval with generation for grounded responses • Two phases: indexing (offline) and query (online) • Measure both retrieval quality and generation faithfulness 2. Pipeline Components • Chunking, embeddings, vector stores, and prompt design • Each component affects downstream quality 104 Key Takeaways (cont.) 3. Agentic Systems • ReAct pattern: alternate reasoning and acting • Multi-tool coordination with observable routing • Comprehensive tracing for debugging and evaluation • Workflows vs agents: choose the simplest architecture that works • Five composable patterns before reaching for full autonomy • Invest in tool

Module 7: RAG & Agentic Pipelines Production AI: An Engineering Guide Theja Tulabandhula University of Illinois Chicago 1 Overview RAG Foundations RAG Pipeline Development Agentic AI Systems Hands-On Demos Lab Exercise Assessments 2 Overview Module Overview How do AI assistants like Perplexity answer questions with up-to-date information they weren’t trained on? • Build Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems • Ground LLM outputs in external knowledge • Create multi-tool agent systems with intelligent routing • Implement observable decision traces for debugging 3 What You’ll Build 1. Complete RAG Pipeline • Document ingestion with chunking strategies • Vector search with FAISS/Chroma • Grounded generation with source attribution 2. Evaluation Framework • Retrieval precision, recall, MRR metrics • Generation faithfulness assessment 3. Multi-Tool Agent • RAG integration as callable tool • Observable decision traces 4 Module Roadmap RAG Foundations (Architecture, Chunking, Embeddings, Vector DBs) ↓ RAG Pipeline Development (Query Design, Context, Evaluation) ↓ Agentic AI Systems (Patterns, Multi-Tool, Agent Integration) 5 RAG Foundations RAG Architecture Overview What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation? • RAG = Architecture pattern enhancing LLM outputs with external knowledge • Retrieved at inference time, not encoded during training • Like an open-book exam : look up specific information when needed Why RAG Matters Problem RAG Solution Hallucination Ground responses in retrieved facts Knowledge cutoff Query current external data Proprietary data Access domain-specific knowledge bases ￿ See: RAG Architecture Overview 6 RAG Pipeline Architecture End-to-End Data Flow Documents → Chunking → Embedding → Vector Store ↓ User Query → Query Embedding → Similarity Search → Retrieved Context ↓ LLM + Context → Grounded Response Two Main Phases 1. Indexing Phase (offline): Process and store documents 2. Query Phase (online): Retrieve and generate responses ￿ See: RAG Architecture Overview 7 RAG Component Responsibilities Component Purpose Example Tools Document Loader Ingest raw documents LangChain loaders, PyPDF Text Splitter Chunk documents RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter Embedding Model Convert text to vectors sentence-transformers Vector Store Store and index embeddings FAISS, Chroma Retriever Find relevant chunks Similarity search, MMR LLM Generate grounded response vLLM, Ollama ￿ See: RAG Architecture Overview 8 Document Chunking Strategies Why Chunking Matters • Embedding models have token limits (256-512 tokens) • Entire documents dilute relevance signals • Chunks must be small enough for precision, large enough for coherence 9 Document Chunking Strategies (cont.) Common Strategies Strategy Best For Drawbacks Fixed-size Uniform processing Ignores semantic boundaries Recursive General documents May split mid-concept Sentence-based Precise retrieval Very small chunks Semantic (headers) Structured docs Requires formatting ￿ See: Document Chunking 10 Illustration: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter( chunk_size=512, # Max chars per chunk chunk_overlap=50, # Overlap between chunks separators=["

", "
", ". " , " " , ""], # Priority order length_function=len ) chunks = splitter.split_text(document_text) 11 Illustration: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (cont.) Key Parameters Parameter Typical Values chunk_size 256-1024 characters chunk_overlap 10-20% of chunk_size separators Paragraphs → Sentences → Words ￿ See: Document Chunking 12 Embedding Models What are Embeddings? • Dense numerical vectors (384-1536 dimensions) capturing semantic meaning • Semantically similar texts imply geometrically close vectors • “automobile” matches “car” even without exact word overlap Popular Models Model Dimensions Speed

results.answer: In a multi-step workflow, an agent should use retrieval to analyze the query, select the appropriate tool (e.g., a retriever), execute it, and trace the steps for debugging. The retrieval process helps in fetching relevant context before generating a grounded response. Insufficient evidence in retrieved context.
